coredata.dc:title: A novel dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe CDs–COO–F for hydrazine detection in aqueous solutions and living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wenfang
Jia, Haishuang
Zhang, Jie
Shao, Ling
Wang, Jun
Fang, Dawei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe, CDs–COO–Fluorescein (CDs–COO–F), for the specific detection of hydrazine (N2H4) was designed and synthesized through esterification reaction of carbon dots (CDs) and fluorescein (F). The prepared CDs were characterized by TEM, XPS, XRD and FT-IR and the optical properties of CDs and CDs–COO–F were described by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. On the other hand, the affecting factors including hydrazine (N2H4) concentration and interfering substances on the hydrazine (N2H4) detection were researched. The high sensitivity and high selectivity of CDs–COO–F in detection hydrazine (N2H4) could be confirmed. Two good linear responses of fluorescence emission intensities of CDs–COO–F at 462 nm and 515 nm as functions of hydrazine (N2H4) concentration were obtained, respectively. The limits of detection hydrazine (N2H4) were 0.504 μM and 0.269 μM under 370 nm and 480 nm excitation wavelength lights, respectively. The detecting mechanism of hydrazine (N2H4) by CDs–COO–F was deduced from the experimental results. Cell imaging and cytotoxicity experiments of CDs–COO–F were measured. The experiment results show that the fluorescent probe CDs–COO–F can be applied to detect hydrazine (N2H4) in aqueous solution and living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescein
Dual-excitation
Dual-emission
Hydrazine detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs–COO–F to hydrazine
Fluorescence spectra of CDs–COO–F solution with different hydrazine concentrations
Selectivity of CDs–COO–F as fluorescent probe in hydrazine detection
Effects of interfering substances on fluorescence spectra of CDs–COO–F + N2H4 solutions
Possible mechanism of detection of hydrazine (N2H4) by CDs–COO–F
Detection of hydrazine in real water samples
Cytotoxicity and fluorescence imaging of CDs–COO–F in living cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Low-resolution TEM image (a), High-resolution TEM image (b), Size distribution histogram (c) and XRD spectrum (d) of prepared CDs.
Infrared spectra of CDs (a) and CDs–COO–F (b).
XPS patterns of (a) CDs, (b) C1s and (c) O1s.
UV–vis absorption spectrum (a) and fluorescence emission spectra (b) of CDs under excitation lights with different wavelengths (340 nm–400 nm).
Fluorescence spectra (a) of CDs, CDs–COO–F and CDs–COO–F + N2H4 (λex = 370 nm) and Fluorescence spectra (b) of fluorescein, CDs–COO–F and CDs–COO–F + N2H4 (λex = 480 nm).

Fluorescence spectra (λex = 370 nm (a) and λex = 480 nm (b)) of CDs–COO–F in aqueous solutions with different concentrations of hydrazine (N2H4) and the linear responses of corresponding fluorescence intensities (λem = 462 nm (a) and λem = 515 nm (b)) as a function of hydrazine (N2H4) concentration.

Fluorescence spectra (λex = 370 nm) (a-1) and (λex = 480 nm) (b-1) and corresponding histograms (λem = 462 nm) (a-2) and (λem = 515 nm) (b-2) of CDs–COO–F in aqueous solutions in the presence of Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Na+, G, NH4 +, Al3+,Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and N2H4.

Fluorescence spectra ((λex = 370 nm) (a-1) and (λex = 480 nm) (b-1)) and corresponding histograms (λem = 462 nm) (a-2) and (λem = 515 nm) (b-2) of CDs–COO–F + N2H4 in aqueous solution in the presence of Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Na+, G, NH4 +, Al3+,Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and N2H4.
The reaction mechanism of CDs–COO–F and hydrazine (N2H4).

Fluorescence spectra (λex = 370 nm) (a) and (λex = 480 nm) (b) of CDs–COO–F in aqueous with different concentration of hydrazine (N2H4) in three water samples (tap water, river water and purified water).
Survival rate of BV2 cells in the presence of CDs–COO–F.

Bright filed (a-1) and blue light field (a-2) images of CDs–COO–F in BV2 cells, bright filed (b-1 and c-1), blue light field (b-2) and green light field (c-2) images of CDs–COO–F + N2H4. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Determined results of hydrazine contents with different concentrations in water samples. (λex = 370 nm. Sample 1: tap water, Sample 2: river water, Sample 3: purified water).
Determined results of hydrazine contents with different concentrations in water samples. (λex = 480 nm. Sample 1: tap water, Sample 2: river water, Sample 3: purified water).
Comparison of the proposed method and previously reported fluorescent probes for the detection of hydrazine.

head.ce:title: A novel dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe CDs–COO–F for hydrazine detection in aqueous solutions and living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Dispersed Element Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyanga, 110036, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Dispersed Element Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Baotou Teachers' College, Baotou, 014030, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Laboratory Medicine, The People's Hospital of Liaoning Province, Shenyangn, 110016, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The People's Hospital of Liaoning Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Laboratory Medicine, The People's Hospital of Liaoning Province, Shenyang 110016, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang, 110036, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe, CDs–COO–Fluorescein (CDs–COO–F), for the specific detection of hydrazine (N2H4) was designed and synthesized through esterification reaction of carbon dots (CDs) and fluorescein (F). The prepared CDs were characterized by TEM, XPS, XRD and FT-IR and the optical properties of CDs and CDs–COO–F were described by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra. On the other hand, the affecting factors including hydrazine (N2H4) concentration and interfering substances on the hydrazine (N2H4) detection were researched. The high sensitivity and high selectivity of CDs–COO–F in detection hydrazine (N2H4) could be confirmed. Two good linear responses of fluorescence emission intensities of CDs–COO–F at 462 nm and 515 nm as functions of hydrazine (N2H4) concentration were obtained, respectively. The limits of detection hydrazine (N2H4) were 0.504 μM and 0.269 μM under 370 nm and 480 nm excitation wavelength lights, respectively. The detecting mechanism of hydrazine (N2H4) by CDs–COO–F was deduced from the experimental results. Cell imaging and cytotoxicity experiments of CDs–COO–F were measured. The experiment results show that the fluorescent probe CDs–COO–F can be applied to detect hydrazine (N2H4) in aqueous solution and living cells.

The novel fluorescent probes for selective detection of hydrazine (N2H4) have attracted more attention due to the application in the real water samples. In this work, a novel dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe for detecting N2H4 was synthesized through esterification reaction of CDs and fluorescein (F). The prepared CDs have a strong fluorescence emission peak at 466 nm under the excitation of 370 nm wavelength light. And that, the fluorescein (F) have a strong emission peak at 515 nm under the excitation of 480 nm wavelength light. When CDs and fluorescein (F) are linked by ester bonds to form CDs–COO–F, the fluorescences of the both are quenched. After adding hydrazine to CDs–COO–F system, the ester bonds are destroyed, which causes the fluorescein (F) to be released and new fluorescent substances (CDs–CO–N2H3) to be generated. Thus, the fluorescences at 462 nm and 515 nm are both recovered. The mechanism of fluorescence responses is proposed. The results show that the CDs–COO–F fluorescent probe can be successfully applied to detect N2H4 in real water samples and BV2 cells.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrazine (N2H4) is an important industrial chemical with strong polarity, corrosivity and reducibility [1–3]. It has been widely used in various fields, such as pharmaceutical, agriculture and chemical industry [4,5]. However, hydrazine (N2H4) is toxic and carcinogenic. And that, because of good water solubility and volatility, it is easily inhaled via mouth and skin. The gradually accelerating mutagenic effects can lead to irreversible damage of organs, including kidneys, liver, lungs, central nerves and respiratory system [6–8]. In the manufacture, transportation, application and disposal of hydrazine (N2H4), a micro leakage can probably cause environmental pollution and threaten human health. Recently, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) identified hydrazine (N2H4) as a potential carcinogen with a threshold limit of 10 ppb [9]. Therefore, it is meaningful to develop an effective and reliable analytical method for detecting trace hydrazine (N2H4) in daily life.

Up till now, some methods for analyzing hydrazine (N2H4) have been exploited, such as electrochemical analysis [10,11], chemiluminescence [12,13] and chromatography [14,15]. Though these methods can be used to accurately detect hydrazine (N2H4), but relatively complicated and time-consuming process as well as special equipments limited their further application. Apparently, it is more necessary to develop a convenient, rapid and low-cost method for detecting hydrazine (N2H4). In general, the fluorescence analysis method, particularly, combined with some fluorescent probes, is considered to be one of the most popular detection methods because of its simplicity, specificity and sensitivity [16]. In fact, some researchers have used fluorescence analysis method to detect hydrazine (N2H4). For example, Zhu et al. reported a novel colorimetric and red-emitting fluorescent probe based on resorufin platform to achieve the detection of hydrazine (N2H4) [17]. However, due to the coexistence of various substances, the detection of hydrazine (N2H4) can easily be interfered by using some single-excitation or single-emission fluorescent probes.

Carbon dots (CDs) as new fluorescence nano materials have been attracting more attention due to the special optical properties since discovered in 2004 [18]. Compared with traditional fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots [19,20], organic dyes [21,22] and rare earth metal nanoparticles [23,24], the CDs have the advantages of excellent water solubility, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [25–27]. The preparation methods of CDs are generally divided into two categories, “top-down” and “bottom-up” [28]. The former uses larger carbon structural substances as raw materials to prepare the CDs by using some methods such as discharge, laser ablation and electrochemical oxidation [29]. And the latter is using molecular organic compounds by combustion, heat treatment and microwave synthesis to prepare CDs [30]. The CDs as electron donor or receptor have a greater ability to couple with many substances, which displays their growing potential values in many applications including sensing and bioimaging. Therefore, the CDs can be used to detect some special substances. Li et al. proposed that the use of CDs from chrysanthemum as carbon precursor could achieve the cursory detection of hydrazine (N2H4) [31]. However, similarly, when these probes were used to detect N2H4, only single excitation wavelength and emission wavelength was relied on to give the responses, which are easily disturbed by some external factors. Therefore, more precise fluorescence probes need to be developed to overcome the disadvantages of single response signal.

In this paper, a new dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe (CDs–COO–F), coupled carbon dots (CDs) with fluorescein (F), for detecting hydrazine (N2H4) was reported. Of which, the CDs were prepared by using “bottom-up” method. The prepared CDs have a strong fluorescence emission peak at 466 nm under the excitation of 370 nm wavelength light. And that, the fluorescein (F) have a strong emission peak at 515 nm under the excitation of 480 nm wavelength light. The CDs–COO–F was synthesized through esterification reaction between CDs and fluorescein (F). When CDs and fluorescein (F) are linked by ester bonds, the fluorescences of the both are quenched, which creates a fluorescent-closed environment, building a chemical bridge between the fluorescent signal of CDs–COO–F and the hydrazine (N2H4). After adding hydrazine (N2H4) to the CDs–COO–F system, the ester bonds are destroyed, which causes the fluorescein (F) to be released and new fluorescent substances (CDs–CO–N2H3) to be generated at the same time. Thus, the fluorescences at 462 nm and 515 nm are both recovered. The detection experiments of hydrazine (N2H4) were performed under the excitation of 370 nm and 480 nm wavelength lights, respectively. The fluorescence intensities of CDs–COO–F is closely related to the concentration of hydrazine (N2H4). The CDs–COO–F has been used to detect hydrazine (N2H4) with low limits of detection (0.504 μM and 0.269 μM). The results showed that CDs–COO–F can be applied to detect hydrazine (N2H4) specifically and sensitively in aqueous solutions and living cells.

CDs were prepared by using citric acid (A.R. Shenyang Chemical Reagent Plant, China). The solution pH was adjusted by using diluted hydrochloric acid (HCl, A.R. Shenyang Chemical Reagent Plant, China) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, A.R. Shenyang Chemical Reagent Plant, China) solutions and controlled by using K2HPO4–KH2PO4 buffer solution (pH = 7.4). CDs–COO–F was prepared by using CDs and fluorescein (Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Co. Ltd.). EDC (A.R. Shenyang Chemical Reagent Plant) and DMAP (A.R. Shenyang Chemical Reagent Plant) were used as the esterification catalyst. All reagents (analytical purity) are obtained from distributors (Shanghai Aladdin biochemical technology, China). All solutions were oreoared by using ultra-pure water (18.3 MΩ cm, Hitech-science tool, Shanghai, China). The BV2 cells were purchased from the Shanghai cell bank of Chinese academy of sciences.

Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, Talos F200X, USA FEI). X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, D-8 Bruker, USA). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos UK). Fourier Transform-Infrared spectra (FT-IR, IRAffinity-1, Japan). Uv–vis absorption spectra (Cary 50, Varian Company, USA). Fluorescence spectra (Cary 300, Varian Company, USA). Fluorescence microscope (LEICA DMI8). Leica biological microscope (LSCM, LEICA DM6000 B, Germany).

Based on the previously reported methods [32,33], the CDs with carboxyl groups were prepared through the pyrolysis of citric acid. The synthesis process is shown in Scheme 1 . In short, 3.20 g of citric acid was put into 25 mL beaker. The beaker was heated to 200 °C using the heating jacket. After 10 min the citric acid melted into a liquid and began to boil. After 15 min, The color of the liquid changed from colorless to orange, and then transferred to a beaker containing 10 mg/mL of aqueous sodium hydroxide solution. The solution was neutralized to pH = 7.4 to get the CDs and stored in a 4.0 °C refrigerator.

The CDs–COO–F was synthesized according to the reported methods [34] (see Scheme 1). The 5.0 mL fluorescein solution was measured, and then transfer to the round bottom flask. EDC and DMAP were added to the flask in the ratio of 4:1, respectively. 2.0 mL CDs solution was measured and slowly added to the above mixed solution. The mixed solution was refluxed at 60 °C for 4.0 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was adjusted to pH = 7.4 by using buffer solution, and stored in an airtight container for later use.

The 1.00 × 10−4 mol L−1 solution was prepared using 80% hydrazine hydrate. 3.0 mL of the above solution was added to 3.0 mL 80 μg mL−1 of CDs–COO–F stock solution. After a period of reaction, 3.0 mL of the mixed solution was taken out, and the fluorescence spectra were measured under excitation wavelengths 370 nm and 480 nm, respectively.

In this experiment, the actual water samples come from river water, tap water and purified water as research objects, respectively. Nine 50 mL volumetric flasks were divided into three groups of three. 3.00 mL 80 μg mL−1 CDs–COO–F reserve solution (pH = 7.4) was added to the above volumetric flask, respectively. Then, the reserve N2H4 solutions with different concentrations were also added to the above of three group volumetric flasks, respectively. These solutions were calibrated with three real water samples to 50 mL. All the experiments are under the condition of room temperature (21.00 ± 0.02 °C).

BV2 cells were subjected to resuscitated and cultured in Ham's F12K (2.0 mM L−1 glutamine, 1.5 g/L NaHCO3) + 15% horse serum + 2.5% fetal bovine serum culture medium. Pour off the culture solution and select adherent cells (live cells). 40 μg mL−1 of the CDs–COO–F was added to the fresh medium, and the cells were incubated in fresh medium for 1.0 h. After washing 3 times with fresh PBS (pH = 7.2), BV2 cells were observed with an invert imaging microscope and photographed.

The CDs were prepared by a fast, simple and efficient “bottom-up” approach. The obtained CDs are characterized by TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS. From Fig. 1 a, it can be seen the prepared CDs with spherical shape are well-separated without any apparent aggregation [35]. The high-resolution TEM image of prepared CDs (in Fig. 1b) shows the well-resolved lattice fringes with an average interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm, which is close proximity to the (102) diffraction plane of sp2 graphitic carbon [36]. According to the TEM measurement, the size distribution histogram is obtained. It can be found from Fig. 1c that the particle size distribution of prepared CDs is mainly in the range of 1.8–3.8 nm. This indicates that the particle size distribution of CDs is relatively narrow and the average diameters are about 2.53 ± 0.3 nm. The above evidence indicates that the CDs have been successfully synthesized [37,38]. As can be seen from Fig. 1d, XRD pattern of prepared CDs also displays a broad peak centered at 23°, which corresponds to the crystal planes (002) of graphene, with lattice spacing of about 0.34 nm. It demonstrates that the CDs are highly amorphous in nature and possess property of the graphite structure [39].

Additionally, FT-IR spectra of CDs (a) and CDs–COO–F (b) were also determined and the results were shown in Fig. 2 . As can be seen from Fig. 2b, the stretching vibration peak of CO in CDs–COO–F appears at 1621 cm−1, which displays a blue-shift by 128 cm−1 compared with 1749 cm−1 of CDs (in Fig. 2a). It is because the formation of the ester bond provides an electron donor group for the CO bond, causing the CO vibration peak to move to short-wave number direction. The asymmetric stretching vibration peak of C–O–C in CDs–COO–F appears at 1394 cm−1, which gives a slight blue-shift by 9 cm−1 compared with 1403 cm−1 of CDs (in Fig. 2a). And the symmetrical stretching vibration peak of C–O–C in CDs–COO–F appears at 1218 cm−1, which shows a blue-shift by 18 cm−1 compared with 1236 cm-1 of CDs (in Fig. 2a). In addition, the stretching vibration peak of C–H of aromatic groups in CDs–COO–F appears at 893 cm−1, which displays a red-shift by 45 cm−1 compared with 848 cm−1 of CDs (in Fig. 2a). The above analyses of FT-IR indicate the presence of abundant carboxylic acid, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs [40]. And the shifts of these characteristic peaks prove that the CDs–COO–F has been successfully synthesized by esterification between CDs and fluorescein (F).

The elemental compositions of CDs were researched by XPS (Fig. 3 ). The XPS survey spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 3a, the two peaks at 284.96 eV and 532.03 eV are exhibited, which principally correspond to C1s and O1s, respectively. In Fig. 3b, the C1s spectrum can be further divided into three peaks at 284.2 eV, 285.8 eV and 288.4 eV, which are attributed to C–C/CC, C–O/CO and C(O)–O [41], respectively. High resolution of the O 1s spectrum (as shown in Fig. 3c) of CDs clearly exhibits two peaks at 531.4 eV and 532.8 eV, which can be assigned to CO/O–H and C–O, respectively. The results are basically consistent with those reported in the previous literature [42]. All results demonstrate that the CDs with good monodispersity and high crystallinity have been successfully prepared.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs in aqueous solutions were shown in Fig. 4 . As can be seen from Fig. 4a, the CDs in aqueous solution give a weak absorption peak at 378 nm, which could be attributed to the π - π* transition of CC or the n - π* transition of CO bond. To address the excitation wavelengths and emission peaks of the prepared CDs, the fluorescence spectra were determined and depicted in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually increased with the increasing excitation wavelengths from 340 nm to 370 nm. The maximum fluorescence intensity at 466 nm was observed under excitation of 370 nm wavelength light. These results are consistent with the previous observations [43]. That is, the emission peak of CDs depends on the excitation patterns. The above analyses of fluorescence spectra can confirm that the best excitation and emission wavelengths of CDs are 370 nm and 466 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs–COO–F to hydrazine
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of CDs, CDs–COO–F and CDs–COO–F + N2H4 solutions were measured under excitation of 370 nm wavelength light. In Fig. 5 a, it shows the CDs in aqueous solution have a strong fluorescence emission peak at 466 nm. After adding fluorescein (F), the fluorescence emission peak of CDs–COO–F is very weak, because of the formation of ester bonds between CDs and fluorescein (F). However, after further adding N2H4, formed CDs–COO–F + N2H4 solution shows a relatively strong fluorescence emission peak at about 462 nm. This is because the ester bonds of CDs–COO–F are broken by hydrazine (N2H4), forming CDs–CO–N2H3, which also have a strong fluorescence emission in aqueous solution. It confirms that CDs–COO–F has potential as fluorescent probe to detect N2H4 upon excitation at 370 nm.

Similarly, the fluorescence spectra of fluorescein (F), CDs–COO–F and CDs–COO–F + N2H4 solutions were measured, respectively, under excitation of 480 nm wavelength light. As shown in Fig. 5b, it can be found that fluorescein (F) solution at 515 nm has a strong fluorescence emission peak. After fluorescein (F) and CDs are mixed together, their fluorescence are both quenched because the CDs and fluorescein (F) are coupled, forming ester bonds. However, after adding hydrazine (N2H4), the CDs–COO–F + N2H4 solutions showed a relatively strong fluorescence emission peak at 515 nm because the ester bonds of CDs–COO–F are broken by hydrazine (N2H4), releasing the fluorescein (F). It can also be confirmed that CDs–COO–F has also a potential to detect N2H4 upon excitation of 480 nm wavelength light. The above analyses indicate that CDs–COO–F as a dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe to detect N2H4. The probe with dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent can be complementary each other, which enhances anti-interference ability and veracity to detection of hydrazine (N2H4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra of CDs–COO–F solution with different hydrazine concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: To research the relationship between fluorescence intensity of CDs–COO–F and hydrazine (N2H4) concentrations, the fluorescence responses of CDs–COO–F (80 μg mL−1) to the different concentrations (0.00 - 10.00 × 10−5 mol L−1) of hydrazine (N2H4) were studied upon excitation at 370 nm and 480 nm, respectively (see Fig. 6 ) CDs and fluorescein (F) are both strongly fluorescent substances and they emit obvious fluorescence, respectively, under their respective excitation wavelengths of lights. When CDs and fluorescein (F) are mixed together, their fluorescence are significantly quenched due to the formation of ester bonds. However, after adding hydrazine (N2H4), the fluorescence of CDs–COO–F solution is increased. This is because the ester bonds of CDs–COO–F are broken by hydrazine (N2H4), forming CDs–CO–N2H3 and fluorescein (F). Moreover, in Figs. 6a–1 it can be found that the fluorescence of CDs–COO–F is gradually increased with the increase of hydrazine (N2H4) concentration. It is due to the increase of the amount of the formed fluorescent substance (CDs–CO–N2H3). According to the relationship between the fluorescence of CDs–COO–F solution and the concentration of hydrazine (N2H4), a linear equation is obtained as shown in Figs. 6a–2. Obviously, under excitation of 370 nm wavelength light, the fluorescence intensity of CDs–COO–F solution showed a good linear correlation in hydrazine (N2H4) aqueous concentration range of 1.50 × 10−7 - 4.50 × 10−5 mol L−1. The linear equation is y = 13.71x + 90.53, with correlation coefficient squared of R2 = 0.9939. Therefore, the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.504 μM. Apparently, the CDs–COO–F, as a fluorescent probe, has relatively low limit of detection (LOD) compared with the previous literature [44,45].

Similar phenomenon can be seen in Figs. 6b–1. The fluorescence of CDs–COO–F gradually increased with increasing concentration of hydrazine (N2H4). This is because the addition of hydrazine (N2H4) can break the ester bonds between CDs and F in CDs–COO–F and release fluorescein (F). As shown in Figs. 6b–2, it is further found that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs–COO–F solution and N2H4 concentrations exhibited a good linear relationship in hydrazine concentration range of 2.00 × 10−7 - 5.00 × 10−5 mol L−1 under the excitation of 480 nm wavelength light. The linear equation is y = 25.68x + 111.09, with correlation coefficient square of R2 = 0.9943. Therefore, the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.269 μM. Apparently, the CDs–COO–F has relatively low limit of detection (LOD) compared with those in previous literatures [46,47]. The above analysis results show that CDs–COO–F not only has a wide detection range, but also a low limit of detection. Therefore, it can be used to detect N2H4 as a dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CDs–COO–F as fluorescent probe in hydrazine detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of CDs–COO–F as fluorescent probe in the detection of N2H4, the fluorescence spectra of CDs–COO–F solutions in the presences of some coexistent substances including Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Na+, glucose (G), NH4 +, Al3+, Cl−, SO4 2- and NO3 − are investigated and the results are given in Fig. 7 . Under excitation of 370 nm wavelength light, the fluorescence intensity of CDs–COO–F is very weak, because of the formation of ester bonds between CDs and fluorescein (F). After adding these coexistent substances, the fluorescence intensity hardly changed, which indicated that added coexistent substances can not destroy the ester bonds between CDs and fluorescein (F). However, after adding hydrazine (N2H4), the fluorescence intensity is significantly increased. This is because the ester bonds of CDs–COO–F are destroyed by hydrazine (N2H4), producing a new fluorescent substance (CDs–CO–N2H3). The above results can be seen more clearly in Figs. 7a–2. The CDs–COO–F has weak fluorescence. However, after adding other coexistent substances, the fluorescence intensity is almost unchanged. Only when Fe3+ ions are added, the fluorescence intensity is reduced slightly. It does not interfere with the detection of hydrazine (N2H4), because hydrazine (N2H4) can obviously enhance fluorescence. It can be demonstrated that CDs–COO–F has high selectivity as fluorescent probe for detecting hydrazine (N2H4) under excitation of 370 nm wavelength light.

Similar phenomenon can be seen in Figs. 7b–1. Under excitation of 480 nm wavelength light, after adding coexistent substances, the fluorescence intensity of CDs–COO–F hardly change. However, after adding hydrazine (N2H4), the significant increase of the fluorescence intensity can be seen because the hydrazine (N2H4) is able to break ester bonds and fluorescein (F) is released. Also, as shown in Figs. 7b–2, it is obvious that the addition of some coexistent substances hardly change the fluorescence intensity. Fluorescence is significantly enhanced when the hydrazine (N2H4) is added in aqueous solution. The above analyses can indicate that the CDs–COO–F has high selectivity as dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe and can be used to detect N2H4, upon excitation of 370 nm and 480 nm wavelength lights, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of interfering substances on fluorescence spectra of CDs–COO–F + N2H4 solutions
ce:section.ce:para: To research the anti-interference property of CDs–COO–F in detection of hydrazine (N2H4), the interfering experiments in the presence of some coexistent substances were performed. The fluorescence spectra of CDs–COO–F solutions in the presences of Zn2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Na+, glucose (G), NH4 +, Al3+, Cl−, SO4 2- and NO3 − are studied and the results are given in Fig. 8 . As displayed in Figs. 8a–1, the fluorescence intensity of CDs–COO–F in aqueous solution is weak under excitation of 370 nm wavelength light, because of the formation of ester bonds between the CDs and fluorescein (F).

However, after adding hydrazine (N2H4), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs–COO–F solution significantly enhanced. This is because the ester bonds of CDs–COO–F are broken by hydrazine (N2H4), forming a new fluorescent substance (CDs–CO–N2H3). After adding other coexistent substances, the fluorescence intensity of CDs–COO–F solutions hardly changed. This indicates that the CDs–COO–F as a fluorescent probe to detect hydrazine (N2H4) is not affected by coexistent substances under excitation of 370 nm wavelength light. The above results can be seen more clearly in Figs. 8a–2. The fluorescence of CDs–COO–F is extremely weak, but after adding hydrazine (N2H4), the fluorescence is significantly enhanced. And that, the addition of other coexistent substances hardly change the fluorescence intensity. It can be confirmed that CDs–COO–F as a fluorescent probe has strong anti-interference ability to detect N2H4 upon excitation of 370 nm wavelength light.

A similar phenomenon can be seen in Figs. 8b–1. Under excitation of 480 nm wavelength light, the fluorescence of CDs–COO–F solution is also weak. After addicting hydrazine (N2H4), the fluorescence intensity is significantly enhanced because the ester bonds are destroyed by hydrazine (N2H4), releasing the fluorescein (F) with strong fluorescence. After adding above coexistent substances, the CDs–COO–F solutions still showed relatively strong fluorescence emission. This indicates that the CDs–COO–F in the hydrazine (N2H4) detection is not affected by these interfering substances under excitation of 480 nm wavelength light. Also, as shown in Figs. 8b–2, it is obvious that the fluorescence intensity of CDs–COO–F is very weak. After adding hydrazine (N2H4) and these coexistent substances, the fluorescence intensity of CDs–COO–F significantly enhanced. This shows that the addition of these coexistent substances hardly influence the fluorescence intensity of CDs–COO–F + N2H4. The above analysis results show that the CDs–COO–F has strong anti-interference ability as dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe in hydrazine (N2H4) detection upon excitation of 370 nm and 480 nm wavelength lights, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of detection of hydrazine (N2H4) by CDs–COO–F
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism on hydrazine (N2H2) detection by using CDs–COO–F as a dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe is described in Fig. 9 . The prepared CDs have a strong fluorescence emission at 466 nm upon excitation of 370 nm wavelength light. Fluorescein (F) contains several aromatic rings, resulting in a strong fluorescence emission at 515 nm upon excitation of 480 nm wavelength light. After the CDs and fluorescein (F) are connected together through esterification reaction, the electrons can be transferred in CDs–COO–F via ester bonds, which destroys the original fluorescence systems of CDs and fluorescein (F). As a result, their fluorescences are both quenched obviously.

However, after adding hydrazine (N2H4) to CDs–COO–F solution, the ester bonds are destroyed and the amido bonds are formed, which causes the fluorescein (F) to be released and new fluorescent substances (CDs–CO–N2H3) to be generated at the same time. In the CDs–CO–N2H3, the –N2H3 is a rigid and small molecule compound and not an aromatic compound. In addition, the CDs–CO–N2H3 is not connected with other aromatic compounds, in which the electrons are difficult to be transferred and transited. So, the generated CDs–CO–N2H3 has a strong fluorescence emission at 462 nm upon excitation of 370 nm wavelength light. When the hydrazine (N2H2) is added into the CDs–COO–F solution, accompanied by the hydrazinolysis of ester bonds, the fluorescences at 466 nm and 515 nm wavelengths are obviously enhanced upon excitation of 370 nm and 480 nm wavelength lights, respectively. On the one hand, the complementation of two excitation wavelength supplies an obvious anti-interference capacity in hydrazine (N2H2) detection. On the other hand, the comparison of two emission wavelengths further provides high veracity and reliability of hydrazine (N2H2) detection. Finally, it is indicated that the CDs–COO–F as a dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe can be applied to detect N2H4 due to the high veracity, sensitivity and anti-interference capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hydrazine in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: For evaluating the feasibility of the mentioned method in real application, the developed fluorescence probe was applied to perform of determination of the hydrazine (N2H4) in tap water, river water and purified water samples adopting the standard addition method and the corresponding recovery values were tested. In Fig. 10 a, it can be seen that the CDs–COO–F solution at 462 nm (λex = 370 nm) has a weak emission peak. After adding water samples containing N2H4, the fluorescence intensities of CDs–COO–F solutions are all enhanced, that further increased with the increase of N2H4 concentration. The fluorescence responses of CDs–COO–F solution containing the same concentration of N2H4 in these water samples were the same and approximate, that demonstrates a good reproducibility with the same hydrazine (N2H4) concentrations, and CDs–COO–F has high accuracy for detecting hydrazine (N2H4). The fluorescence spectra of CDs–COO–F displays good layering for different hydrazine (N2H4) concentrations, possesses good dispersibility with the different hydrazine (N2H4) concentrations, and CDs–COO–F has high sensitivity to detection of hydrazine (N2H4). This also indicates that the ester bonds of CDs–COO–F can be destroyed by hydrazine (N2H4) in different water samples, resulting the new fluorescence to achieve the detection of hydrazine (N2H4).

Upon excitation of 480 nm wavelength light, similar phenomenon can be seen in Fig. 10b. After adding these water samples containing hydrazine (N2H4), the fluorescence intensities of CDs–COO–F solutions are all enhanced. As the increase of the N2H4 concentrations in three aqueous samples, the fluorescence intensity of CDs–COO–F gradually increased. The fluorescence spectra of CDs–COO–F shows better overlapping for same hydrazine (N2H4) concentrations and display a good upward gradient along with the increasing concentration of N2H4. It indicates that the CDs–COO–F has high accuracy and high sensitivity for the detection of hydrazine (N2H4). It further demonstrates that ester bonds of CDs–COO–F can be destroyed by hydrazine (N2H4) in different water samples, the fluorescein (F) to be released to achieve the detection of hydrazine (N2H4). The above analysis results show that the CDs–COO–F can be used as a dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe detect N2H4 in various water samples upon excitation at 370 nm and 480 nm, respectively.

The results of recovery rates tests by using the standard addition method were shown in Table 1 and Table 2 , respectively. The good recovery rates are achieved from 91.0% to 105.5% for three different water samples. In addition, the results of comparison of the proposed method with previously reported probes for detecting hydrazine (N2H4) are shown in Table 3 . Therefore, These results indicate that CDs–COO–F could quantitatively detect the trace amount of hydrazine (N2H4) in actual water samples upon excitation at 370 nm and 480 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and fluorescence imaging of CDs–COO–F in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: It is great significance to study the cytotoxicity of the fluorescent probe on detecting intracellular hydrazine (N2H4) in practical application. The effects of different concentrations of CDs–COO–F on cytotoxicity were investigated by an MTT assay on BV2 cells.

As shown in Fig. 11 , it can be seen that the survival rate of BV2 cells without CDs–COO–F is 100%. The survival rate of cells slightly decreased with the increase of hydrazine (N2H4) concentration. Even the CDs–COO–F was added with the concentration is 320 μg mL−1, the survival rate is found to decrease only 18%. It is proved that CDs–COO–F has low toxicity. Based on the above results, it can be predicted that the CDs–COO–F is promising as a fluorescent probe to detect hydrazine (N2H4) in cells due to its lower toxicity.

Subsequently, the fluorescence imaging experiments of CDs–COO–F in cells were performed to further explore the potential application in hydrazine (N2H4) detection and the obtained results were shown in Fig. 12 . As shown in Figs. 12a–1, the BV2 cells could normally grow up even in the presence of CDs–COO–F in bright field. However, the size and shape of this group of BV2 cells were different from those of the other groups of BV2 cells treated with CDs–COO–F + N2H4 (as shown in Figss. 12b–1 and Figs. 12c–1). It suggests that the presence of hydrazine (N2H4) affects the normal growth of BV2 cells to some extent. As can be seen from Figs. 12a–2, for the BV2 cells treated with CDs–COO–F the fluorescence was hardly observed in the blue channel. It indicates that the BV2 cells treated with CDs–COO–F can not give the observable fluorescence image without hydrazine (N2H4).

For the BV2 cells treated with CDs–COO–F upon the addition of hydrazine (N2H4), under the irradiation of 405 nm wavelength light the strong blue fluorescence could be observed in the blue channel around 462 nm wavelength (Figs. 12b–2), compared with that of the BV2 cells treated with CDs–COO–F (Figs. 12a–2). Apparently, it is attributed to the reaction of N2H4 with intracellular CDs–COO–F. Similarly, under the irradiation of 488 nm wavelength light, the strong green fluorescence could be observed in the green channel around 515 nm wavelength (Figs. 12c–2), compared with that of the BV2 cells only treated with CDs–COO–F (Figs. 12a–2). Obviously, the ester bonds of CDs–COO–F in the BV2 cells have been destroyed by hydrazine (N2H4) molecules. Based on the above analyses, it can be predicted that the CDs–COO–F as a fluorescent probe has the potential to detect hydrazine (N2H4) in cells.

In summary, CDs–COO–F as a dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe has been developed for hydrazine (N2H4) sensing. For CDs–COO–F, the complementation of two excitation wavelengths displays obvious anti-interference capacity in hydrazine (N2H4) detection. And the contrast of two emission wavelengths gives high sensitivity and high veracity. Under excitation of 370 nm and 480 nm wavelength lights, the detection ranges are 1.50 × 10−7 - 4.50 × 10−5 mol L−1 and 2.00 × 10−7 - 5.00 × 10−5 mol L−1, respectively, and the corresponding limits of detection (LOD) are 0.504 μM and 0.269 μM. The further experimental results indicate that the CDs–COO–F can be used as a dual-excitation and dual-emission fluorescent probe for the detection of hydrazine (N2H4) in real water samples. Moreover, the CDs–COO–F as a fluorescent probe has low cytotoxicity and displays the potential to detect hydrazine (N2H4) in cells.
Wenfang Liu: Investigation, Data Curation, Formal analysis, Writing - Review & Editing.
Haishuang Jia: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data Curation, Formal analysis, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing.
Jie Zhang: Investigation.
Ling Shao: Investigation.
Jun Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources.
Dawei Fang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China NSFC (No. 21673107), Liaoning BaiQianWan Talents Program (2017), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1807197), Key Technologies R & D Program of Liaoning Provincial Department of Education (2019), Program for Liaoning Innovative Research Team in University (No. LT2019006) and Project of the Central Government in Guidance of Local Science and Technology Development (2020). This work was also financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (21371084) and the Innovative Team Project of Education Department of Liaoning Province (LT2018018) of China.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel ratiometric and colorimetric ﬂuorescent probe for hydrazine based on ring-opening reaction and its applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shi XR, Yin CX, Zhang YB, Wen Y, Huo FJ. A novel ratiometric and colorimetric ﬂuorescent probe for hydrazine based on ring-opening reaction and its applications. Sens Actuator B 2019;285:368-374.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A smart ﬂuorescent probe for discriminative detection of hydrazine and bisulﬁte from different emission channels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu JJ, Pan J, Ye Z, Zeng LT, Su DD. A smart ﬂuorescent probe for discriminative detection of hydrazine and bisulﬁte from different emission channels. Sensor Actuator B 2018;274:274-284.
